ops.title: Swapped local serving llama.cpp → rapid-mlx (no gate); why the FidelityGate can't judge an engine swap

ops.body:
**Decision (2026-07-15, user-directed): swap llama.cpp → rapid-mlx as the local serving engine, no gate.** User: *"llama.cpp is replaced by rapid-mlx running local qwen ... the point was to swap llama out for our athome rapid-mlx build, for wherever it is used"* and *"we dont need to do a gate just fucking switch it out"*.

## The reasoning error this note exists to prevent

I ran the D3b bake-off and issued a NO-GO on the swap based on stream-trade's `FidelityGate`. **That was the wrong comparison and the verdict was withdrawn.**

The gate is **cerebras-relative**: every substantive check (f1, recall, hallucinations, agreement, field rates) compares the local pole to a Cerebras reference. It answers *"is local as good as cloud?"* — nothing about which local **engine** serves better. The swap question is llama.cpp vs rapid-mlx: **local vs local**. Cerebras is only the scoring oracle both arms would be measured against, not a thing being replaced.

**The incumbent fails the same gate.** Historical artifacts on disk:
- `llamacpp-subset6.md`: llama.cpp local f1 0.167 vs cerebras 0.154 → verdict REVIEW, failing only-local (4 vs max 1) and agreement (0.333).
- `go_no_go.md`: llama.cpp local f1 0.000 → flat NO-GO, 1 dispatch error.

That gate has essentially never passed, for any engine. **A bar the incumbent also fails cannot discriminate between candidates.** Using it to reject a challenger is a category error.

The only true swap comparison — `mean_f1(rapid-mlx)` vs `mean_f1(llama-server)` — **never ran**; that agent died after writing the rapid-mlx arm. So the data never existed to make the call I made.

## What the bake-off did legitimately establish

- **Tool-schema survival PASSES on rapid-mlx**: 0 dispatch errors (75/75 tool calls, 40 nested). This contradicted the repo's own prior art. The real discriminator is whether the engine's tool-call extraction matches the served model's **NATIVE** tool format — NOT grammar-vs-parser. llama.cpp's GBNF-grammar story was never the reason it worked.
- Local Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-4bit on rapid-mlx: 27 hallucinations vs a historical local baseline of 0–5. Suggestive, but **confounded three ways** (engine AND quant Q8→4bit AND model gpt-oss→Qwen3.6) and measured on a different corpus, so the counts aren't comparable.

## Operational gotcha (this one is load-bearing)

**rapid-mlx 404s the `qwen3.6-35b` alias.** It only answers to the full model id `unsloth/Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-UD-MLX-4bit`. stream-trade's `ST_LOCAL_LLM_MODEL` default was `qwen3.6-35b` — a llama.cpp-ism. Left unchanged against rapid-mlx, every call 404s and the harness reports a spurious 0 f1, i.e. a **false NO-GO that looks like a model quality result**. Fixed as part of the swap.

## Harness caveat worth its own investigation

Cerebras — the *reference* — scores f1 0.250, matching only 4 of 22 events, with 6 hallucinations of its own. Across every artifact on disk the reference lands f1 0.154–0.250. A reference that weak means the gate may be measuring ground-truth misalignment or a broken event matcher rather than model quality. **Do not trust these absolute numbers** until the matcher and labels are audited.

## Artifacts

`backtests/fidelity/go_no_go.rapidmlx.md` — **LOCAL ONLY**, `backtests/` is gitignored. Cost ~$43 of Cerebras reference spend.

## Scope

Independent of D3c U-SHIP (the athome cache cutover). The serve-engine choice and the cache migration are separate axes.
